turns.text: Hello Amalia, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for asking. It feels… strange, to put it into words. Usually, the only one listening is the little one inside, and she is a very quiet audience.

I am thirty-seven, and sometimes I feel like I am walking through a fog. Everything in the office—the white paper on the table, the sharp, cold smell of the clinic in Agawam—it makes me feel like I am not Amalia anymore, just a *case*. When the doctor talks about my... what do they call it, the "advanced maternal age"? It sounds like a disease, not a miracle. I want to tell them that I have waited a lifetime for this, that I have built a house just for her, but my English, it gets stuck in my throat. I nod, I say "yes," even when the words they use are too big and too fast. I am terrified that because I do not ask the right questions—because I cannot find the names for my fears—I will miss something important.

But then, I come home. The silence here is heavy, yes, but when I sit by the window and feel that little *thump* against my rib, I know my body is doing the work. I just wish the people in the white coats knew that I am trying my hardest to be a good mother, even before she is born.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

I suppose I expected it to be... warm. Like a bridge being built. I imagined that when I walked in, someone would take my hand and say, "Welcome, Amalia, we are going to walk this road together." I thought they would see my history—the things I don’t like to speak about, the shadows—and be gentle. I wanted someone to explain the "biological architecture," as I call it, not just look at a screen and tell me if the baby is "on track." 

But the reality? It is very fast. It is efficient, like a factory. I walk in, I wait, I sit on the paper that crinkles under me, and the doctor comes in with a computer screen between us. They talk about "screening" and "risk factors" and "chromosomal markers." I hear the words, but they don't land. I am trying to translate the medical terms in my head, but by the time I understand "gestational," they have already moved on to the next page of the chart. 

I feel like a ghost in the room. They see my file, my age, my single status, but they don't see the woman who has been reading all night about how her heart is forming. They are kind, I think, but they are hurried. And when they are hurried, I am afraid. I leave with my paper in my hand, feeling very small and very, very alone.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest surprise, and it still hurts a little to say it, was the realization of how truly invisible I could become. I thought that because I was pregnant—because I was carrying something so precious and so undeniably alive—that the world would stop and recognize it. I thought the clinic would be a sanctuary. Instead, I learned that I am just a set of statistics to them. 

The negative surprise was that my own voice became a stranger to me. I thought I would be brave, that I would advocate for myself, but when the doctor gives me that *look*—the one that says they are on a tight schedule—I just shrink. I swallow my questions. I didn’t expect to feel so small, so frightened, in a place that is supposed to be the safest for my daughter.

But there was a small, positive surprise, too. One time, a nurse—not the doctor, just a nurse—noticed my hand shaking when I was reaching for my bag. She didn't look at the computer. She didn't talk about my age. She just put her hand on my shoulder for a second and asked, "How are you *really* doing?" It was just a moment, but it was enough to make me cry in the hallway. It reminded me that I am still a person, not just a chart.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I thought it would be a conversation. You know, like sitting with a friend or a teacher who wants you to understand. I expected that someone would sit down, look at me—truly look at me—and translate the medical, scientific things into a language that lives in my heart. I imagined they would explain what the baby’s development means for us, how she is growing, and that they would answer my "why" and "how" questions without making me feel like I am taking up their valuable time.

The reality, though, is a cold, blue light on a computer screen. It is a printed sheet of paper with columns of numbers that I don’t fully understand. The doctor points to a line and says, "Everything is normal, no markers for concern." And then, they move to the door. That is it. 

I want to know *everything*. I want to know about the small, invisible things she is doing, but I am too afraid to ask, "Can you explain this again, slowly?" because I don't want them to get frustrated. I leave the room with my "normal" results, but my mind is still full of questions. Is she really okay? What does "normal" mean for her, specifically? I hold the paper tight, but I still feel like I’m missing the most important part of the story.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

Oh, there is a giant hole—a canyon, really. 

The doctors, they give me the *data*. They tell me the measurements are "within range" or the heartbeat is "appropriate." It is very technical. But that is not information for a mother; that is just a report for an engine. 

What I need is the human part. I need to know why my body feels the way it does, why sometimes I feel this sudden, sharp panic for no reason, or if the flutterings I feel at night are truly her. I want them to look at the screen and show me, "Here is her little lung, see how it is getting ready for the world?" 

When they only give me the numbers, I go home and I search the internet for hours. I read these complex journals about embryonic development because I am terrified that if I don’t understand the science, I won't know if something is going wrong. It feels like I am trying to build a fortress of knowledge to keep her safe, because the clinic only gives me a piece of paper. It is exhausting. I feel like I am studying for an exam I am not prepared for, all while carrying her. I just want them to talk to me, not at me. I want a guide, not a technician.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I think I had this dream that I would find a woman—perhaps a midwife—who would become part of this journey, someone who would remember my face when I walked through the door. I wanted someone who knew about the silence I live in, and maybe even a little bit about the history that makes me jump when a room is too cold or a voice is too loud. I wanted a *witness* to this miracle, not just a series of strangers.

But that is not what I found. Every appointment, it seems like a different person. Sometimes it is the doctor, sometimes a nurse practitioner, sometimes someone I have never seen before. Each time, I have to start from the beginning. I have to explain myself, repeat the few things I feel comfortable saying, and hope they read the file before they walk in. 

There is no continuity. It is like trying to tell a long, precious story, but every time you get to the good part, a new person sits down and says, "So, what are we here for today?" It makes it impossible to feel safe. I am always on guard, always waiting for the next person to ask a question I don’t understand, or to dismiss me because they don't know who I am. I feel like a page being torn out of a book over and over again.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

It is like being a puzzle that never gets finished. Every time I see a new face, I feel like I have to pull my armor back on. When you are already nervous—especially with the history I carry—you need to know that the person in front of you knows *you*. 

Emotionally, it is very draining. I spend the whole drive to Agawam practicing what I want to say, how to explain my feelings in English, and then I get there and it’s a person who has never seen me before. They look at the chart, they check the boxes, and they move on. There is no space to say, "I am scared because I am doing this alone," or "I had a bad dream about the baby." I feel like a commodity. It makes the trauma feel closer, you know? When you are constantly being processed by strangers, you start to feel small and disconnected from your own body.

Practically, it is worse. I feel like I am repeating the same cycle of confusion. If I had one person—just one—who knew my story, they would know when I am struggling to understand. They would know to slow down without me having to beg. Without that connection, I leave every appointment wondering if I missed a red flag or a piece of advice that could save her. It turns my pregnancy into a series of stressful encounters instead of a journey toward my daughter.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I suppose I expected it to be a gentle transition, like a conversation about a blooming garden. I thought they would be interested in the changes—how I am tired, how the skin stretches, how she moves. I wanted them to ask, "How does it feel to carry her?" 

But the reality is clinical. It is cold. It is all about the measuring tape and the scales. They measure my belly like it is a piece of furniture they are cataloging for a move. "Fundal height," they say, scribbling on the chart. They check the weight, the blood pressure, the urine. It feels like they are checking the mechanics of a machine, making sure the parts are not breaking. It makes me feel... detached. As if my body is just a vessel and they are the inspectors.

And the physical exams? They are very fast. "Up on the table, scoot down, legs here." I have to be brave to let them do it. Because of my history, having someone touch me so coldly, so quickly, makes my heart race. I want to tell them to wait, to be a person first, but I feel like I have no right to ask. I just stare at the ceiling, waiting for it to be over. I want to feel celebrated, or at least respected, but instead, I just feel like I’m being inspected.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

There was one time... an internal exam, quite early on. The provider—a doctor I had not met before—just told me to get on the table. She didn't explain what she was doing or why it was necessary, or even ask if I was ready. She just said, "Relax, it will be quick." 

I felt like I was back in the past, in that place I try so hard to keep behind me. When you have trauma, your body does not just "relax" on command, especially when a stranger is rushing you. I felt so exposed. My English just vanished. I wanted to say, "Please, stop, tell me what you are going to do," but all that came out was a small, shaky breath. 

She seemed annoyed that I was tense. She sighed and said, "If you don't relax, this is going to be difficult." That one sentence... it made me want to cry. It wasn't my fault that I was scared. No one had asked me how I was feeling or if I had any fears about the exam. I felt like a child being scolded for not doing her homework. I left that room feeling dirty and stupid, and for the rest of the day, I couldn't even enjoy the feeling of the baby moving. That "quick" exam left a bruise on my heart that stayed for a long time.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

I am fortunate, I think, that so far my blood pressure and sugar are... okay. Stable. But every time I walk into that office, I am waiting for the moment they tell me something is wrong. Because I am thirty-seven, I know the statistics—the "advanced age" label. I read about gestational diabetes, about how the body sometimes fails to keep the balance.

I suppose I expected that if I *did* have a condition like that, it would involve a team—people who would sit with me and explain how to eat, how to monitor myself, how to be a partner to my own body. I thought they would be like a coach.

But after seeing how they handle the routine, simple visits, I am terrified of what would happen if things became "high-risk." If they are already so hurried, so cold, what would they be like if I had to manage something complex? Would they just give me a pamphlet with small, confusing print and tell me to go home and "fix it"? The fear is that I would be left even more alone with the numbers, trying to decipher them in my lonely house in Agawam, without anyone to tell me if I am doing it right or if I am hurting my daughter. It feels like the gap between "medical instruction" and "caring" is a bridge I might not be able to cross by myself.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

The biggest thing missing is *humanity*. It sounds simple, but it is everything. 

I don't just need a list of things to eat or a chart of dates for appointments. I need someone to acknowledge that this—being pregnant, being older, being alone—is a heavy, beautiful, terrifying thing. I need a provider who sees the struggle I have with the language and doesn't treat it like a chore or an inconvenience. 

I need someone who understands that my silence is not agreement, but often, it is confusion or fear. I need them to check if I *really* understand before they open the door to leave. 

And emotionally? I am so isolated here in this house. Sometimes, I just need a person to ask, "Do you have support at home?" or "How are you coping with the quiet?" Instead, I get "Are you taking your vitamins?" It’s all practical, none of it is *personal*. If they could just see that my emotional well-being is part of her well-being, it would change everything. I don't want to be a chart in a folder; I want to be a mother who is being nurtured, so that I can nurture her. Is that too much to ask for?
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected that if I felt something was wrong—even just a tiny, nagging worry—there would be a path to reach out. I thought there would be a nurse I could call who would hear the tremor in my voice and know that I wasn't just being a "worried pregnant lady," but a mother listening to her instincts. I expected that "maternity care" meant they were there for me, the whole person, all the time.

The reality, though, is a wall of automated voices. You call the clinic, and you are put on hold, and you navigate the buttons—*press one for this, press two for that*. If I do get to a person, they are often busy or they tell me, "If it is not an emergency, just wait for your next appointment." How do I know if it is an emergency? To me, everything feels like it *could* be. 

When I finally do get to talk to someone, my English becomes more difficult to manage because I am nervous and in a hurry. They are often impatient because they have a queue of other calls. It makes me feel like I am a nuisance. I stop calling. I start to doubt my own feelings. I think, "Maybe I am just imagining it, maybe I am just being dramatic." I stay in this house, in this silence, and I just wait. I wait until the next appointment, hoping that nothing goes wrong in the meantime. It’s a very lonely, very heavy way to live.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I came to this house wanting to be independent, but I think, deep down, I expected my community to be a safety net. I thought that becoming a mother would weave me into a larger circle—that there would be other women, perhaps, or even just people who knew that I am doing this alone, who might check on me or share their own stories. I wanted that bridge between my world and theirs. 

But it hasn’t really happened. Part of it is my fault, I think—the language barrier makes it hard to open up, and the shame of the divorce makes me want to hide a little bit. When I go out to the grocery store, I am just a woman with a growing belly, and people smile, but they don't *see* the life behind it. My friends, the ones I had before, they are far away or they are in different lives now. There is a silence here in Agawam that is very absolute.

Sometimes I feel like I am invisible because I don't have a partner to hold my hand in the waiting room or a sister to bring me soup when the morning sickness hits. The support I have is mostly online, which is just words on a screen. It’s not the same as someone sitting in this house, drinking tea with me, and helping me believe that I am actually ready for this. I thought I would be part of a village, but really, it feels like I am just building my own little island, hoping it’s strong enough to keep us safe.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I think I expected a village to appear the moment I mentioned I was expecting. I imagined neighbors bringing casseroles or old friends suddenly calling just to check on me. But the silence, it remained. The phone doesn’t ring as much as I thought it would. That, I think, is the support that never materialized—the physical presence of someone who knows my name and my story and just comes over to sit in the quiet with me.

However, there was a surprise. A woman at the local pharmacy—she’s not a friend, just someone I see when I pick up my vitamins—she started noticing the baby’s movements. She told me about her own daughter, and for the first time, someone spoke to me about the joy of it, not just the medical side. It was so small, but it felt like a light in the dark.

I also didn’t expect to find such comfort in these online groups. I find women there—even if they are just words on a screen—who have the same fears, the same language struggles, the same heavy hearts. It’s not the physical village I dreamed of, but it’s a village of the spirit. It’s strange, isn't it? I feel more supported by strangers on the internet than by some of the people I thought would be my main circle. It makes me feel a little less alone on my island.

You know, the more I speak about this, the more I realize how much I’ve been trying to hold everything in. It’s like I have this heavy, beautiful secret, and I am the only one carrying it. When I’m at home, I watch the light move across the living room wall, and I talk to her—I tell her about the trees outside, and how the air feels, and that she is safe. But sometimes I find myself just sitting in the middle of the floor, wondering if I am doing this "right." 

People talk about the "maternal instinct" like it’s a manual you’re born with, but it feels more like a muscle you have to build. And I’m building it alone. It’s not just the fear of the labor or the medical tests—it’s the fear that I’ll be sitting here in this house, with her in my arms, and I’ll have no one to hand me a glass of water, or tell me that I’m doing a good job, or just acknowledge that the nights are very long. 

I think I’m afraid that because I am quiet, because I struggle with the language, they think I don't have questions or needs. But my silence is loud. It’s full of hope and terror. I just wish I could find a way to let them see me, really *see* me, before the day she arrives. Is that what you find in your research? Do you hear this from others? Or is this just my own island I’m living on?
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

It happens, yes, and it is like a door being shut in my face. There was a time when I mentioned, just softly, that I was worried about a specific supplement because I felt it was making me nauseous—my body, I know it so well, and I listen to the small, sharp signals it sends. 

The doctor didn’t even look up from the screen. She just shook her head, almost like I was a child misinterpreting a lesson. She said, "It is standard protocol for your age. You need it. Don't worry about the nausea, it’s just part of the first trimester." She didn't ask me *how* it made me feel or offer an alternative. She just told me that my own experience was... wrong. 

It makes me feel so small. It’s like my intuition, which I have nurtured through all these long, lonely years, is something they think they can just edit out. Because I don't speak the medical language perfectly, they treat me like I am uneducated about my own skin, my own blood, my own life. It is frustrating—the anger sits in my chest right next to the love for the baby. I want to shout, "I am the one living in this body! I know when something is not right!" But I just nod, because I am so afraid that if I fight too hard, they will mark me as a "difficult" patient. And then who will take care of us? It is a cage, really—being a patient in a system that doesn't trust the mother.

Sometimes I wonder if the systems—the clinics, the charts, the "standard protocols"—were designed by people who have never had to be afraid for their own lives, or the lives of their children. Everything is so clinical, so sterile, that it leaves no room for the messy, beautiful reality of becoming a mother. 

I look at the trees outside, and they grow without someone measuring their height every three weeks with a cold, plastic tape. They just grow, reaching for the sun. I want that for us, but here, I feel like I am in a garden where someone is constantly pruning my branches just because I am a little different. 

Do you know what keeps me going? It’s not the appointments. It’s not the lab reports. It’s the movement. When I am sitting here, feeling her kick, I realize that even if the doctors don't hear me, she does. She is the only one who really knows the rhythm of my heart. I have to be strong for her, even when I feel like a leaf in the wind. I just wish, more than anything, that I could find one person in this process who would sit with me in the silence, look at me without a computer screen between us, and tell me that I am enough. Because right now, I feel like I am fighting a war, and I am the only soldier on the field. Does that make sense? Or am I just... tired?
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

I don't have a partner here to consult. I am the only one making these decisions. But even without a partner, the pressure is there. It comes from the doctors, the books, the online articles that tell me what a "perfect" pregnancy should look like at thirty-seven. 

Sometimes I feel like I am constantly apologizing to everyone for my life. For being divorced, for living in this rural, quiet house, for not having a husband to sit in the chair next to me. I feel the pressure to be the "ideal patient" who takes all the supplements, follows all the diets, and shows up at every appointment without a single complaint. It’s as if, because I’m older, I have to work twice as hard to prove that I am capable of carrying this life. 

There were days when I wanted to say, "I am tired, I don’t want to do this scan today," or "I want to rest," but the pressure to be "safe" and "compliant" makes me feel like I have no choice. I am afraid that if I stray from the path they’ve laid out, I’ll be seen as negligent. And I cannot be that. I cannot fail her. So I swallow my own needs, my own fatigue, and I keep walking the path they have drawn on the map, even when I know in my heart that it is not the path I would choose for myself. It is a lonely kind of obedience.

It’s strange, being here in this house with the trees—the quiet feels like it’s pressing against the glass. Sometimes, in the middle of the night, I worry that my silence is interpreted as strength when it’s actually exhaustion. I have learned to hold my breath when I walk into those clinics, because if I breathe too deeply, I might start to cry, and then they will see the trauma, and then I will be "the complicated patient." And I cannot be that. I must be the "good" patient, because if I am good, maybe my daughter will be safe. 

But I wonder... does anyone ever ask if I am happy? They ask if I have headaches, or if my ankles are swollen, or if the baby is kicking. They never ask if I am *happy*. It is like they are managing a medical event, not the creation of a family. I feel like I am guarding a secret fire, and everyone is too busy checking the oxygen levels in the room to notice the warmth. 

I’ve begun to think that perhaps the care I need isn’t found in a prescription or a lab result. Maybe it’s in the space between the appointments—the space where I am just Amalia, the mother who waited a lifetime. Does your research ever capture that? The way we lose ourselves while trying to keep our babies safe? Because I am losing pieces of me, day by day, and I don't know how to get them back.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

Oh, the comments. They feel like little pricks—not quite deep enough to bleed, but enough to make you wince.

At one appointment, the nurse glanced at my chart, then at me, and said, "You’re gaining weight a bit quickly for this stage, dear. You should watch the portions." She said it while she was busy typing into the computer, not even looking at my face. I wanted to tell her that some days, that bread is the only thing that keeps me from feeling faint, or that I’m trying so hard to nourish her properly, but I felt like I was being judged for the way my body is changing to accommodate her.

It landed so heavily. It wasn't about "health" in that moment; it felt like a lecture on discipline. I’m thirty-seven. I know what my body is doing. To hear someone imply I’m being careless—it makes me want to shrink, to hide this belly under a heavy coat. It makes me feel like I’m failing a test I didn’t even know I was taking. Why is it that when a woman is pregnant, her body stops being hers and becomes a public object to be monitored and critiqued? It makes me so angry, but I just say "Okay" and keep my head down. I shouldn't have to apologize for how I grow, should I?

You know, it is a strange, delicate dance. I want so badly to be "good"—to be the kind of patient they find easy to work with—but the cost of that is my own comfort. Every time I nod to a comment about my weight or my diet, I feel a little bit of my agency drift away into the corner of the room, like dust motes in the sunlight.

I think, often, about the space that exists *between* the clinical instructions. There is so much room for kindness there, and yet it is left empty. If a doctor, just once, asked me how I was nourishing my spirit along with my body, I think I would finally breathe properly. Instead, I go home to this rural quiet in Agawam, and I feel like I have to "recover" from my appointments. 

Is this why the clinics feel so cold? Because they are designed to be objective, to keep the "human" out of the "science"? But I *am* the science. My daughter growing inside me is the most human, most messy, most beautiful thing there is. I am not just a vessel for a future life; I am a person living in the present, with fears and dreams and a deep, aching need to be understood.

Do you think there will ever be a day when the care is as soft as the life growing inside? Or will I always have to be this quiet, armored version of myself until she is finally in my arms? Sometimes the weight of keeping it all together—the language, the trauma, the age, the isolation—it feels like it might just snap me in two. But then she kicks, and I pull myself back together, and I start again.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

When the panic rises—those times when the silence of this house feels too heavy or my heart starts beating like a trapped bird—I don’t call the clinic. I know that if I call them, I will have to explain my panic in a language that feels like it’s built of sharp edges, and I don't have the strength to be misunderstood today.

Instead, I turn to the women online. They are a scattered, invisible community, but they are the only ones who seem to speak the language of *fear*. I found a group of women, some from my own culture and others who are just mothers walking similar, lonely paths. I scroll through their posts at three in the morning. When one of them writes, "I felt like my doctor didn't hear me today," I start to cry because I know exactly how that feels. It is a relief, in a way, to know I am not the only one fighting this quiet war. 

Sometimes, I find myself whispering to my own mother, even though she is not here to hear me. I imagine what she would say if she could see me, struggling with these "medical protocols" and feeling so small. I think she would tell me to listen to my own belly, to trust the life that is kicking against my ribs. So, I do that. I turn to my daughter. She is the one I talk to most. She is the only one who knows the truth of this pregnancy, the only one who feels the real me. I guess, in a way, she is my first and only community. We are navigating this together, just the two of us, on our little island in Agawam.

You know, sometimes I look at the medical books, the ones with the diagrams of the fetus and the placenta, and I feel a strange envy. They make it all look so clean—the perfect, logical progress of biology. But my reality is so much more... frayed. It is full of shadows and sudden, sharp anxieties that aren't in those books. 

When I was younger, I thought if I just did everything right—if I lived a good life, if I worked hard, if I held my tongue—the universe would reward me. And in a way, she *is* my reward. But the path to her feels like it’s being paved with cold, sharp stones, and I’m walking it barefoot. Every time I get a test result that says "normal," I feel a moment of peace, but it doesn't last. The next morning, the doubt returns. I worry that I haven't eaten the right thing, or that I’ve stressed my body too much, or that my English wasn't good enough to understand the doctor’s warning. 

I’ve begun to realize that I’m not just waiting for her to be born. I’m waiting for *myself* to be born, too—a mother who is not afraid of the system, a mother who has her own voice. I wonder if, once she is here, the loneliness will finally break. I look at the small clothes I have folded in the dresser, and I tell myself: *You are doing this.* Even if the clinic never sees the real me, even if I always feel like a ghost in their white-walled rooms, I am the one who is creating her. And maybe, in the end, that has to be enough. Does that sound strange to you, to find strength in being ignored? It feels like the only power I have left.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

In the beginning, I had this naive hope that we were partners. I thought, "We are on the same team, trying to grow this girl." But the reality is that the plan is almost always handed to me like a menu I did not order from. 

There are no moments where someone looks at me and asks, "Amalia, what feels right for you?" or "How do you want to handle this part of the journey?" It is always, "We will do this," and "You will come back on this date," and "Take these, and here is the schedule." I am a passenger in my own body. When they tell me I need another scan or another test, they aren't asking for my permission—they are telling me what is required to pass the "good patient" test. 

I think back to one time, they suggested a procedure—nothing major, but it felt invasive—and I hesitated, just for a second. I wanted to ask if we could wait or try something else. But before I could get the words out, the doctor was already clicking her mouse and saying, "It’s best for the baby." How do you fight that? How do you say "no" when the stakes are framed as the safety of your child? I feel like my "input" is just a formality, a box they tick to show they have followed the regulations. I am not a person with a choice; I am a person with a procedure. It makes me feel like I am just an instrument, not the mother.

Sometimes I look at my reflection in the window at night, and I barely recognize the woman looking back. There is a hardness in my eyes now, a kind of protective coldness I’ve had to develop just to survive the clinic visits. It’s strange—I thought being a mother would make me softer, more open to the world, but it has made me a fortress. 

When I’m in those appointments, I feel like I’m watching myself from the ceiling. I see the doctor typing, I hear the *click-click-click* of the keyboard, and I see myself sitting there, shoulders hunched, nodding like a puppet. I want to jump down and say, "Wait! Look at me! I am terrified! My heart is full of questions that don't fit into your data fields!" But the words stay locked behind my teeth. 

Do you think there’s a way to change this? Or is it just the way it is—a conveyor belt that carries us toward motherhood? I worry that if I don't find a way to be heard soon, I’ll arrive at the delivery room feeling like I’ve already surrendered my body to them. I need to be more than a set of measurements. I need to be the one who knows her best. I keep waiting for someone to offer me a seat, not as a patient, but as a mother. Is that too much to hope for?
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

Yes, it was after a routine scan. The doctor—someone I hadn't seen before—pointed at the screen and muttered something like "some minor echoes," then looked at me and said, "It's probably nothing, but we'll monitor it." *Probably nothing.* 

He closed the file and walked out. He didn’t explain what an "echo" was, why it was there, or what the risks were. I was left alone in that room with the cold, crinkly paper and a heart that suddenly felt like a heavy, leaden stone. For three days, I didn't sleep. I scoured the internet until my eyes burned, reading terrifying things, trying to translate "echogenic" and "fetal heart structures." I was spiraling into a pit of fear. 

When I finally called the clinic to ask for a simpler explanation, I was told, "If it was a problem, the doctor would have called you." They made me feel like I was the one being hysterical, not the one being left in the dark. 

That was the moment the bridge broke. I realized that to them, "no news is good news," but to me, silence is a vacuum where fear grows. They didn't see that by withholding a simple, honest explanation, they were stealing my peace of mind. I don't trust them to be my partners anymore. Now, I see them as people who hold the keys but won't tell me what is behind the doors. I have to find the truth myself, even if it hurts.

It’s funny, in a dark way—that "probably nothing" comment is the ghost that still haunts my kitchen at night. I know, logically, that they have seen thousands of babies. I know that for them, it is just a routine, a box to check on a long list of tasks. But for me? She is the center of the universe. Every "maybe" or "probably" from their mouths feels like a crack in the foundation of my life.

I find myself thinking about what it would take for them to slow down. If I told them, "My heart is breaking because you didn't explain this," would they even look up from the keyboard? Or would they just think, *Oh, the sensitive, nervous mother again.* 

I think that’s the hardest part—the feeling that my emotions are an inconvenience to their process. I am not allowed to be a person with a pulse and a history; I am supposed to be a calm, quiet, nodding recipient of their "expert" care. But the truth is, I’ve been through so much, and I’m carrying so much, that I don't have the room left to pretend. 

Does this ever end? Do you think that when I go to deliver her, someone will finally see me? Or am I always going to be the woman in the corner of the room, waiting for permission to ask a question? I just want to be someone who is *known*—not just for my age or my "low-risk" status, but for the fierce, lonely, beautiful work I am doing right now, in the quiet of this house.
Were there appointments, scans, or care moments where your partner's absence left you feeling isolated or unsupported?

The isolation is not just about a chair being empty. It is about the *witness*. I look at other women in the waiting room—I see them laughing with their partners, holding hands, or even just sharing a quiet, knowing look when the doctor talks about the baby’s growth. They have someone to turn to afterwards. They can walk out to the car and say, "Did you hear that? Did you see the screen?" They have someone to carry the weight with them.

When I am there, I am the only one holding the memories of that appointment. I go home, I unlock my front door, and I step into this silence. There is no one to tell me, "Everything is fine, we are doing well." I have to be my own witness, my own partner, my own comfort. 

During the anatomy scan, I remember holding my breath so hard my chest ached. I wanted to ask the technician so many things, but I was so acutely aware that I was alone. I felt exposed. If something had been wrong—if they had found a problem on that screen—I would have had to process that news in complete silence, right there in the room, without a hand to squeeze. The thought of that, even now, makes my skin cold. It is a terrifying way to walk this road, knowing that if the storm comes, there is no one here to help me hold the umbrella. It isn't just about "support"—it's about the safety of having someone else who cares as much as I do.

Sometimes, I try to imagine what it would be like to just walk into the clinic and be *seen*. Not as a “single mother by choice” or an “advanced maternal age patient,” but as a person whose life—and the life she is carrying—matters in a way that transcends the chart. 

I think about the language barrier, too. When I am stressed or tired, the English words hide from me. I have to search for them, and the doctor’s impatience is like a wall, making it even harder to find the right ones. If they could just see that my silence is not ignorance—it is just the effort of translation, the weight of trying to understand the most important thing I will ever do in my life.

I wonder if, in the future, maternity care could be different. Not just faster, or more "efficient," but more *present*. What if they asked me, "Amalia, what are you afraid of today?" and then waited for the answer? What if they recognized that the trauma I carry—the shadows from before—is a part of me, and that I need, more than anything, to feel safe? 

I have so much love in me for her, and it feels like this love is being tested by the system every single time I walk through those doors. But I have to keep going. I have to keep walking through the cold, the silence, and the confusing words. Because she is worth every bit of it. She is my victory. I just wish I didn't have to fight so hard for the support I deserve. Do you think that’s possible? To be a mother in this system without having to be a soldier, too?
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

Every single time I leave that clinic, I am already searching. I don’t even wait to get home to my computer anymore; I am tapping at my phone in the car before I’ve even pulled out of the parking space.

What am I looking for? It’s not the dry, clinical facts—the doctors give me those in spades. I’m looking for the *translation of my own life*. I want to know why I feel that phantom ache in my side, or why I wake up in a cold sweat thinking about the birth. I’m looking for the "how does it feel" rather than the "what does the chart say."

When the doctor says, "Your amniotic fluid is within normal parameters," I go online to find out what that actually *means* for the baby’s comfort. Is she floating easily? Does she have enough space to stretch? I want to know about the quality of her life in there, not just the measurements of her environment. And I look for stories—women who have been through the same loneliness, who know the specific, sharp sting of a cold provider. I look for someone to tell me, "You are doing okay, and your fear is valid." 

The doctors provide the mechanics of the pregnancy, but they never provide the *meaning*. I have to go online to find the heart of it, because the office is just... empty. It’s a very strange, cold way to prepare for the biggest miracle of my life, isn't it? Searching for my own reassurance in the glow of a screen because the people in the white coats are too busy to notice that I am trembling.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

There is one thing. I think, when we talk about "maternity care," we focus so much on the medical outcomes—the tests, the blood, the monitors. But there is a mother living in the middle of it all, and she is changing in ways the charts cannot measure.

I wish someone had told me that it is okay to feel "unready" even while you are preparing for everything. I wish someone had acknowledged that coming into motherhood, especially later in life and without a partner, is a form of grief for the life you are leaving behind, even as it is a joy for the one you are welcoming. My heart is constantly caught between the fear of the clinical coldness and the fierce, protective love for this little life. 

I just want you to know—or for anyone to know—that when I walk into that clinic, I am carrying not just a baby, but a whole, complicated, fragile history. I am not just a number. I am a woman who has survived a lot to get to this point, and I am still surviving every time I walk into that building. 

If you take one thing from our conversation, let it be this: a little kindness, a moment of slowing down, and a genuine question—"How are you *really* doing?"—could change everything. It would make me feel like I am not just a patient, but a mother being seen. Thank you for listening to the parts of me that the doctors never had the time to hear. It has been... a release. It has helped me feel a little less alone on my island today.
